title: The Purple-Seller's House
subtitle: Psates son of Abraamios, the Village of This, 592--616 CE
summary: In 592 CE Psates and Maria sold a crumbling house in This to their son Pachymios. The later papers show why they did it, and what that house helped him build.

sections.title: What the deed secured
sections.body_html:
Psates and Maria were people from Panopolis who had lived in This for a long time.9 Panopolis was a cloth city, and the trade name that sits so casually in the deed, purple-seller, points back toward that larger textile world.1012 The family still held property there when the next paper in the archive was drawn up.11

That next paper came eight years later. By 14 July 600 CE both parents were dead.13 Their children divided the estate: Pachymios, Ioannes, and their sister Tarsene.14 The deed calls it a small estate.15 It still had enough in it to divide carefully. Movable property, immovable property, and self-moving property were all counted out; two men named Chariston, one a copper-worker and one an ironworker, appear among the assets.1617

Then the earlier sale comes back into view. Ioannes and Tarsene formally acknowledged that they had no claim against Pachymios or his heirs over the house their parents had sold him while alive.18 If they challenged that sale later, the penalty would be twelve gold nomismata.19

The house had cost just under three.1 Reopening the matter would cost four times that.

sections.title: What Pachymios did with it
sections.body_html:
The later papers show Pachymios working from a more secure position than his siblings.

In 605 he hired a purple-worker named Dioskoros for two years.20 Dioskoros was to work alongside Pachymios’s other hired workers.21 The wages were paid in grain, nineteen artabas across the term, with food provided by the employer.22 If the worker walked out early, he owed a gold nomisma. If Pachymios dismissed him early, Pachymios still owed the full wage.23

A year later a man named Kallinikos borrowed from Pachymios and promised to repay in wine at the vintage.24 In 613 Kallinikos borrowed again on the same pattern.25 The house sale had not made Pachymios rich. It had helped place him where a workshop, credit, and property could stay in the same hands.

sections.title: The lane in This
sections.body_html:
In 616 he bought another house in the village.26 The sellers were women who had inherited it, and the property stood on the lane of the guard-house.27 The price was thirteen keratia.28 One boundary of the new purchase already touched the house of the buyer Pachymios.29

The archive ends there: a house sold by parents to a son in 592, a sibling settlement in 600 that locks the sale in place, a workshop contract, two wine loans, then another house next door.

The papers never say whether Ioannes resented the arrangement, whether Tarsene argued before she agreed, or whether everyone in the family knew from the start that Pachymios was the child best placed to hold the property together. They do show the mechanics. Two parents who could not write paid for a proper deed, a proper valuation, and proper title papers so that one weak piece of the estate would not be torn apart later. Their children honored that decision, and Pachymios kept building from it.

sources.translation: [...] in the reign and consulship of our most divine lord of the inhabited world, Flavius Mauricius, the eternal Augustus and Imperator, year ten, Payni 20, beginning of the 11th indiction, in the village of This in the Theban province. The Aurelii Psates son of Abraamios, whose mother is Tmouthes, purple-seller, and his wife Maria, daughter of Philamon, whose mother is Taniskene, all from the city of Panopolis, but now for a long time resident in the village of This of the Thinite nome, providing on their behalf a signatory and witnesses of this perpetual sale, those who sign below, we set forth this deed of sale as a simple written guarantee, to abide by this lawful sale and not to violate it in perpetuity; to Aurelius Pachymios, our most sweet son, himself also a purple-seller, from the same village, greetings. By which we acknowledge of our own free will and voluntary decision and guileless conscience and unwavering judgment and unrepentant choice, with firm and right mind, with the fullest right of ownership and with every authority for perpetual possession, that we have sold and conveyed in good faith to you today, having been persuaded, the property belonging to us: a dilapidated house and a cell together with the bare land, with all their rights, situated in the same village on the lane called that of the heirs of Pachot and the oil-press of the heirs of Pachareabe, having come to us by purchase from Viktor son of Psaios and Senouthes son of Phaliout, in accordance with the force of the two old sale documents which are also deposited with you by us. The boundaries on all sides of the aforesaid house and bare land and cell are as follows: to the south, the oil-press of the heirs of Pachareabe; to the north, adjoining the house of Pebos son of Horos; to the west, the house of the heirs of Viktor the priest and of Pebos son of Horos, into which the door opens; to the east, the public lane — or whoever the neighbors may be on all sides, from the ground to the sky. And so that you, the purchaser Pachymis, may own and control these and have full ownership, and improve and build, and sell and resell if you wish, and bequeath to heirs and successors and assigns, and donate and make use of them in whatever manner you wish for all time in perpetuity. The price mutually agreed upon and approved, upon the assessment of Senouthes the builder from the same village: three imperial simple tested gold nomismata less one keration, by the weight-standard of Phylloph(?), total: 3 gold nomismata less 1. Which complete price we, the sellers Psates and Maria his wife, have received from you the purchaser Pachymios, by hand-to-hand payment from the house, in full count, by which we have been persuaded. The guarantee of these things being provided by us and by our heirs and successors and assigns, with every guarantee, against everyone who may make a claim, whether on account of our name or public obligation or private debt or anything else whatsoever at any time, of necessity. And if it should happen — which may it not — that any eviction or dispute should arise concerning these matters, then we the sellers and our heirs and successors and assigns at our own expense shall ward off and repel all claimants or challengers, or else to repay the aforesaid price in double together with all expenses and costs spent on the improvement of the buildings; and for any legal proceedings, if they should arise, to restore the like in double to you, and further to pay as a penalty two simple gold pieces, while this sale remains unbreakable and unshakeable in perpetuity, which we have set forth for security, being valid and firm wherever it may be produced. And having been asked the formal question, we so agreed. We, the Aurelii Psates and Maria his wife, the aforementioned, have made this sale willingly and having been persuaded, and all the written terms are acceptable to us, as stated above. I wrote on their behalf at their request, Psates son of Papnouthios, deacon, from This. I, Besatas son of Elias, deacon, from This, witness the sale, having heard from those who made it. I, Athanasios son of Paamios, priest, from This, witness the sale, having heard from those who made it. Written through me, Ioulios, notary.
